name: Sãotomense (Forro / Santome)

allophone_rules.notes: Nasality in Santome is bi-phonemic: an oral vowel + a nasal coda consonant; the nasal feature spreads leftward onto the vowel and the coda deletes (Araujo & Agostinho 2010, pp. 65, 69: ALUSTP writes nasal vowels as V+n, or V+m before p/b — tason /tasõ/ 'to sit', tamen /tamẽ/ 'big', mpon [mpõ] 'bread'). ALUSTP marks no phonemic nasal vowels apart from lexicalized ũ (ũa 'one').
See CRI_NASAL_A. tason /tasõ/ 'to sit' (Araujo & Agostinho 2010, p. 66).

The coda nasal deletes after transferring its nasality to the preceding vowel (Araujo & Agostinho 2010, p. 65: bi-phonemic nasality — the nasal consonant is not itself realized; lexical items end in an oral or nasal vowel).

REDECLARES the inherited pt-PT rule id with Santome-specific grounding: the only licit non-nasal coda in Santome is the voiceless palato-alveolar fricative, written ⟨x⟩ in ALUSTP (mlaxka 'mask'); a coda sibilant surfaces as [ʃ] — cf. stlijón [ʃtliʒõ] 'healer' (Araujo & Agostinho 2010, pp. 55, 67).
DISABLES the inherited pt-PT coda /z/ → [ʒ]: Santome licenses no /z/ coda (codas are a nasal or /ʃ/ only; Araujo & Agostinho 2010, p. 67). Identity rewrite cancels the inherited rule by id.

DISABLES the inherited EP dark coda [ɫ]: no source describes velarization for Santome, whose codas are a nasal or /ʃ/ only (Araujo & Agostinho 2010, p. 67). Identity rewrite keeps [ɫ] out of the inventory.

ancestors.notes: Portuguese superstrate: lexifier from the 1490s settlement of São Tomé; core vocabulary and much surface phonotactics restructured onto substrate patterns.

Kikongo (Bantu) substrate: Hagemeijer (2011, JPCL 26:1) characterizes the Gulf of Guinea creole substrate, in particular of Santome, as 'halfway Edo and Kongo'. Contributes prenasalized onsets and the strict (C)V template.
Edo/Bini (Edoid, Niger-Congo) substrate: primary substrate of the proto-Gulf-of-Guinea creole (Hagemeijer 2011; Hagemeijer & Ogie 2011). Source of the labial-velar stop /ɡ͡b/ and prenasalized stops.

notes: Sãotomense / Forro / Santome. Portuguese-based Gulf of Guinea creole of São Tomé island (São Tomé and Príncipe), the LARGEST Gulf of Guinea creole (~70,000+ speakers). Sister of Principense (pre), Angolar (aoa) and Fa d'Ambô (fab), all descending from the 16th-century proto-creole of São Tomé. This spec targets the ALUSTP standard orthography (Alfabeto Unificado para as Línguas Nativas de S. Tomé e Príncipe), the standard used by the Araujo & Hagemeijer (2013) Dicionário livre santome. KEY FEATURES: (1) SEVEN ORAL VOWELS /i e ɛ a ɔ o u/ — ⟨ê ô⟩ are close-mid /e o/, bare ⟨e o⟩ are open-mid /ɛ ɔ/ (Araujo & Agostinho 2010, p. 65). (2) NASALITY IS BI-PHONEMIC: oral vowel + nasal coda; the coda deletes and nasalizes the vowel (written V+n, V+m before p/b); the tilde appears only in lexicalized ũ (ũa 'one'). Ferraz (1979) and APiCS instead analyse five phonemic nasal vowels — CONTESTED; this spec follows ALUSTP. (3) PRENASALIZED ONSETS mb/nd/ng/mp word-initially, which vary freely with plain oral stops (ngembu ~ gembu 'bat'); ALUSTP treats them as complex onsets, not unit phonemes. (4) LABIAL-VELAR /ɡ͡b/ ⟨gb⟩ (gbêgbê 'snail sp.'), an Edoid substrate retention; /k͡p/ belongs to Principense/Angolar, NOT Santome. (5) AFFRICATES/SIBILANTS: tx /tʃ/, dj /dʒ/, x /ʃ/, j /ʒ/. (6) SINGLE RHOTIC: one alveolar tap /ɾ/ ⟨r⟩; no tap/trill contrast; /r/ varies freely with /l/ for some speakers (Fontes 2007). (7) CODAS: only a nasal or /ʃ/ ⟨x⟩ (mlaxka 'mask'). (8) IMPLOSIVES /ɓ ɗ/ posited by Ferraz (1979)/APiCS are NOT phonemes of the ALUSTP analysis and have no ALUSTP grapheme — documented, not emitted. (9) STRESS/TONE CONTESTED: Ferraz analyses stress, Maurer/APiCS argue tone; ALUSTP marks neither (the circumflex marks vowel height, not stress), so word stress is NOT orthographically recoverable and this spec does not assign it.

sources.author: Araujo, G. A. de & Agostinho, A. L. dos S.
sources.title: Padronização das línguas nacionais de São Tomé e Príncipe
sources.notes: Open access; reproduces the ALUSTP alphabet table verbatim (pp. 66-67): seven vowels, ê/ô vs e/o, x/j/tx/dj/nh/lh/gb graphemes, V+n nasality, single tap r; bi-phonemic nasality analysis (p. 65); prenasal ~ oral free variation (pp. 68-69).

sources.author: Araujo, G. A. de & Hagemeijer, T.
sources.title: Dicionário livre santome-português / Livlu-nglandji santome-putugêji
sources.notes: The dictionary that adopts and disseminates the ALUSTP orthography this spec targets.

sources.author: Ferraz, L. I.
sources.title: The Creole of São Tomé
sources.notes: Classic phonology; analyses five phonemic nasal vowels, implosives /ɓ ɗ/, stress, and vowel harmony (p. 25) — positions this spec records as contested where ALUSTP differs.

sources.author: Hagemeijer, T. & Maurer, P.
sources.title: Santome (APiCS Online, survey chapter 35)
sources.publisher: In: Michaelis et al. (eds.), Atlas of Pidgin and Creole Language Structures, OUP
sources.notes: Inventory incl. implosives and prenasals; palatalization of /t d s z/ before close front vowels; tone analysis; vowel-elision sandhi (mata ê → mat'e).

sources.author: Hagemeijer, T.
sources.title: The Gulf of Guinea creoles: genetic and typological relations
sources.publisher: Journal of Pidgin and Creole Languages 26(1)
sources.notes: Substrate 'halfway Edo and Kongo'; genetic unity of the four Gulf of Guinea creoles.

orthography_standard.name: ALUSTP — Alfabeto Unificado para as Línguas Nativas de São Tomé e Príncipe
orthography_standard.authority: Governo de São Tomé e Príncipe
orthography_standard.notes: Proposed in Araujo & Agostinho (2010) and adopted (Diário da República de STP, 2013); used by the Araujo & Hagemeijer (2013) dictionary. Phonemic design: ê/ô close-mid vs bare e/o open-mid; x /ʃ/, j /ʒ/, tx /tʃ/, dj /dʒ/, nh /ɲ/, lh /ʎ/, gb /ɡ͡b/; nasality written V+n (V+m before p/b); no stress or tone marking.
